coredata.dc:description: Periodontitis, a common chronic inflammatory disease, is characterized by T cell responses leading to gradual alveolar bone loss and eventual tooth mobility or loss. Herein, polylysine-derived carbon quantum dots (PLL-CQDs) are synthesized via the pyrolysis method to facilitate the recovery of periodontal bone loss induced by periodontitis by modulating T cell phenotype. The PLL-CQDs, characterized by small particle size (2.31 ± 0.70 nm) and fluorescence properties, preserved the positive charge of PLL, affirming successful preparation. In vitro studies revealed that PLL-CQDs significantly increased T cell proliferation in a concentration-dependent manner, while effectively suppressing differentiation into CD4+ phenotype. Furthermore, they promoted alkaline phosphatase expression and calcium nodule formation in MC3T3-E1 cells, indicating potential for periodontal bone regeneration. In vivo experiments demonstrated that PLL-CQDs mitigated alveolar bone resorption and modulated periodontal tissue inflammation. Overall, PLL-CQDs exhibited the ability to regulate T cell differentiation and expedite periodontal bone healing, offering promising implications for periodontitis treatment and suggesting novel strategies for addressing immune-associated bone diseases using CQDs.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Periodontitis, a common chronic inflammatory disease, is characterized by T cell responses leading to gradual alveolar bone loss and eventual tooth mobility or loss. Herein, polylysine-derived carbon quantum dots (PLL-CQDs) are synthesized via the pyrolysis method to facilitate the recovery of periodontal bone loss induced by periodontitis by modulating T cell phenotype. The PLL-CQDs, characterized by small particle size (2.31 ± 0.70 nm) and fluorescence properties, preserved the positive charge of PLL, affirming successful preparation. In vitro studies revealed that PLL-CQDs significantly increased T cell proliferation in a concentration-dependent manner, while effectively suppressing differentiation into CD4+ phenotype. Furthermore, they promoted alkaline phosphatase expression and calcium nodule formation in MC3T3-E1 cells, indicating potential for periodontal bone regeneration. In vivo experiments demonstrated that PLL-CQDs mitigated alveolar bone resorption and modulated periodontal tissue inflammation. Overall, PLL-CQDs exhibited the ability to regulate T cell differentiation and expedite periodontal bone healing, offering promising implications for periodontitis treatment and suggesting novel strategies for addressing immune-associated bone diseases using CQDs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Introduction
ce:section.ce:para: Periodontitis, a prevalent chronic inflammatory disease affecting the supporting structures of the teeth (i.e., periodontium), is characterized by progressive tooth loosening and subsequent loss [1–3]. The destruction of periodontal tissue results from an imbalance in the host immune mechanism, particularly involving the accumulation of macrophages and T cells [4,5]. Severe periodontitis generally causes systemic conditions such as cardiovascular disease, diabetes, rheumatoid arthritis, and Alzheimer's disease [6,7]. Recent immunotherapy approaches for periodontal inflammation include using proanthocyanidins to modulate macrophages and reduce periodontal inflammation, as well as resveratrol to lower reactive oxygen species (ROS) levels and mitigate oxidative stress [8]. These strategies underscore the clinical significance of developing new therapies that modulate the immune response for treating periodontitis. T cells play a crucial role in periodontal tissue destruction, with T cell differentiation closely linked to osteogenesis and osteoclast activity [9–11]. For example, activated T cells can interact with osteoblast-expressed molecules and regulate intraosseous homeostasis by producing osteoclast-related cytokines such as nuclear factor-κB ligand, receptor activator, and tumor necrosis factor-alpha [12,13], impacting bone regeneration processes [14]. Therefore, strategies aimed at controlling T cell-mediated immune responses have garnered significant attention as a means to enhance defective periodontal bone healing in cases of periodontitis.

Recently, the clinical strategies for treating periodontitis primarily involve mechanical debridement, anti-inflammatory drug application, and regenerative procedures [15–17]. While these methods demonstrate therapeutic efficacy in the early stages, they are limited by challenges such as difficulty in accessing lesions without specialized instruments, potential for drug resistance development, and insufficient consideration of the role of T cell-mediated immune responses in periodontitis. Consequently, long-term treatment often results in unsatisfactory periodontal bone healing and may lead to treatment failure [18]. Given the nanoarchitecture's capacity to modulate T cell activation and differentiation in immune responses [19,20], various nanoparticles have been developed and widely utilized to enhance periodontal bone regeneration due to their ability to access lesion sites effectively [21–23]. Among them, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have emerged as a promising option for periodontitis treatment owing to the wide availability of precursor sources for CQD synthesis and the preservation of essential (bio)physicochemical properties from precursors, enabling efficient bone regeneration and immune regulation [24–26]. For instance, CQDs derived from zinc gluconate precursors have been shown to enhance osteogenesis by activating the mitogen-activated protein kinase signaling pathway via ROS, while CQDs prepared from aspirin have exhibited anti-inflammatory effects by reducing prostaglandin E2 levels in an acute inflammation animal model [27,28]. Polylysine (PLL), a positively charged molecule facilitating cell binding, has demonstrated potent antibacterial properties by disrupting bacterial cell membranes, boosting alkaline phosphatase (ALP) expression to promote osteogenic regeneration, and offering immunotherapeutic benefits for gastric diseases through T cell differentiation regulation, suggesting potential for periodontitis treatment [29–31]. Since CQDs retain precursor characteristics, we propose that utilizing PLL to develop novel CQDs for modulating T cell-mediated immune responses could represent an effective strategy for enhancing the healing of periodontitis-induced periodontal bone defects.

To verify this hypothesis, a novel type of CQDs were synthesized by pyrolyzing PLL in this study (Fig. 1 ). Comprehensive characterization of the resulting PLL-based CQDs (PLL-CQDs) was conducted to verify the physicochemical properties of the CQDs and the retained chemical features of PLL. Subsequently, the impact of PLL-CQDs on T cell differentiation was evaluated through cell viability assays, flow cytometry analysis, and spleen coefficient measurements. Then, the influence of PLL-CQDs on alkaline phosphatase (ALP) expression and calcium nodule formation was examined to assess osteoblast differentiation in MC3T3-E1 cells induced by PLL-CQDs. Finally, a proof-of-concept evaluation was performed in an in vivo SD rat periodontitis model by administering 200 μg/mL sterile PLL-CQDs for 28 d. This was followed by Masson's trichrome staining, hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining, micro-computed tomography (Micro-CT) imaging, osteoclast staining, and immunohistochemical analysis of inflammatory markers to confirm the ability of PLL-CQDs to modulate T cell-mediated immune responses and promote periodontal bone healing in periodontitis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Materials and methods
PLL-CQDs
Fabrication of PLL-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: PLL-CQDs were synthesized using a pyrolytic procedure according to previous studies [32]. Briefly, PLL powder (Molecular weight ∼ 4000, Dipper Chemical Technology, China) was heated at 240 ℃ for 3 h in a box muffle oven. After natural cooling to room temperature, the residue was ground into a powder and dissolved into double distilled water (DDW) at a centration of 50 mg/mL. Then, the mixture was sonicated under 40 kHz for 60 min, centrifuged at 11,000 rpm for 30 min, and dialyzed using a dialysis tube (Molecular weight cutoff = 5000 Da, Shanghai Yuanye Bio-Technology, China) for 24 h. Lastly, the samples were lyophilized under a freeze dryer (Alpha1-2 LD plus; CHRIST®, Germany) to obtain PLL-CQDs powder.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterizations of PLL-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: To analyze the physicochemical properties of CQDs, the morphology of the obtained PLL-CQDs dispersed in ultrapure water was examined by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM JEM-2100F, Japan). The size distribution (n = 30) of the sample was measured using ImageJ software from HRTEM images. Then, the ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra of PLL-CQDs were detected from 200 to 600 nm using an ultraviolet spectrophotometer (Shanghai Yuanxi, China). Furthermore, the fluorescence spectroscopy of PLL-CQDs was also performed with a fluorescence (FL) spectrophotometer (MAPLETTA UV-6300, UK), and the emission spectra were recorded in the wavelength range of 300–480 nm.

To explore the remained chemical characteristics of PLL, the zeta potential of PLL and PLL-CQDs was obtained through dynamic light scattering at 25 ℃ using the instrument Zeta sizer (Nano ZSE, UK). In addition, the functional groups of PLL-CQDs were further analyzed by Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FT-IR Nicolet iS10, Thermo Scientific, USA) in the wavenumber range of 500–3800 cm−1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of PLL-CQDs on T cell differentiation
Isolation and culture of mouse T cells
ce:section.ce:para: Spleens were dissected from C57BL/6 mice. This study was approved by the Ethics Committee of Qingdao University (approval number: 202205SD40202304033). After mechanical grinding, the splenocyte mixture was obtained by filtration. Then, the suspension was treated with a mouse T cell isolation kit (StemCell, USA) to obtain T cells. During cell proliferation, T cells (105 cells/mL) were cultured in 1640 RPMI (Solarbio, China) complete medium (with 10 % FBS) in the presence of anti-mouse CD3 and anti-mouse CD28 antibodies (Peprotech, United States) in a 5 % CO2 incubator at 37 ℃.

ce:section.ce:section-title: T Cells viability affected by PLL-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The viability of T cells was assayed using Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8, Meilunbio, China). Briefly, T cells (105 cells/well in 96-well plates) were co-cultured with 0, 50, 200, and 800 µg/mL PLL-CQDs for 3 and 5 d. Then, the cells of every well were incubated with 10 μL of CCK-8 solution at 37 °C for 4 h at each time point. Lastly, the absorbance measurements at 450 nm were tested to analyze the cell number using a microplate reader (SynergyH1/H1M, Bio-Tek, China).

ce:section.ce:section-title: T Cell differentiation affected by PLL-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
To investigate the effect of PLL-CQDs on T cell differentiation. Six C57BL/6 mice were randomly divided into 2 groups (i.e., the Control group and the PLL-CQDs group). And caudal vein injection with 50 mg/kg PLL-CQDs, and equivalent phosphate buffer saline (PBS), once a day, respectively [33]. Execution by intraperitoneal injection of 0.3 % sodium pentobarbital on the 1st d and body weights of the mice were measured. Spleen samples were surgically removed immediately, and the spleen coefficient was calculated.

After the spleen was separated, the T cells were screened according to the previous method of mouse T cell isolation and culture, and appropriate amounts of Anti-Mouse CD4, phycoerythrin (PE, Clone GK1.5), and Anti-Mouse CD8α, phycoerythrin-cyanine 7 (PE-Cy7, Clone: 53–6.7) (Multi Sciences Biotech, China) were added. The supernatant was discarded and resuspended with a flow-through staining buffer and then assayed on the machine.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of PLL-CQDs on the osteogenic differentiation of MC3T3-E1 cells
MC3T3-E1 cell culture
ce:section.ce:para: MC3T3-E1 (a murine osteoblastic cell line) cells were supplied by the Cell Culture Centre of Shanghai Institutes for Biological Science Chinese Academy of Sciences. MC3T3-E1 cells were incubated with Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium (DMEM) supplemented with 10 % (v/v) FBS and 1 % penicillin–streptomycin (Meilunbio, Dalian, China) solution at 37 °C in 5 % CO2 and during cell culture the medium was changed every 2 d. When MC3T3-E1 cells grow to achieve 80–90 % confluence, the cells are dissociated and collected for cell passage or subsequent experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Osteogenic differentiation of MC3T3-E1 cells induced by PLL-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: MC3T3-E1 cells were seeded at a density of 2 × 104 cells/well in 24-well plates and incubated with the osteogenic induction medium (the basic culture medium with 0.1 mM dexamethasone, 10 mM β-glycerol phosphate, and 50 μg/mL L-ascorbic acid). To evaluate the effect of PLL-CQDs on the osteogenic differentiation of MC3T3-E1 cells, the osteogenic induction medium (Control), the 200 μg/mL PLL-CQDs in 1640 RPMI complete medium (PLL-CQDs), the T cells (T Cells), the T cells co-cultured with 200 μg/mL PLL-CQDs (P + T), the supernatant of T cells co-cultured with 200 μg/mL PLL-CQDs (s-P + T) were added into the medium of MC3T3-E1 cells, respectively. After being cultured for 7 and 14 d, the early osteogenic differentiation capacity of MC3T3-E1 cells was estimated using an ALP staining kit (Meilunbio, Dalian, China) by following the manufacturer’s instructions. Briefly, the incubated samples were washed 3 times with PBS and then fixed with 4 % paraformaldehyde (Biosharp, China) for 2 min. After that, the samples were co-cultured with an ALP staining kit for 4 h and the images were captured by an inverted microscope (Nikon A1 MP, Japan). To further detect the quantitative expression of ALP, an ALP assay kit (Nanjing Jiancheng Bioengineering Institute, China) was applied by following the manufacturer’s instructions. To standardize, the total cell protein concentration of samples was measured by a BCA protein assay kit (Meilunbio, Dalian, China). ALP activity was shown as total cellular protein content (king unit/g prot).

To examine the late osteogenic differentiation capacity of MC3T3-E1 cells, the mineralization of the extracellular matrix was analyzed through alizarin red staining (ARS) (Beyotime, China) after 14 and 21 d of culture. Briefly, the incubated samples were washed 3 times with PBS and fixed in 95 % ethanol for 30 min. After removing the fixative, the samples were rinsed 3 times with distilled water. Then, the samples were stained with 2 % alizarin red (pH 4.2) for 30 min at 37 °C and the images were captured using the inverted microscope (Nikon A1 MP, Japan). To quantify the degree of mineralization by calcium accumulation, stained samples were eluted with 10 % (m/v) cetylpyridinium chloride (Meilunbi, China) for 1 h, and the released alizarin red was analyzed using a spectrophotometric instrument at 562 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Animal experiments
The toxicity of PLL-CQDs in vivo
ce:section.ce:para: One day after caudal vein injection of PLL-CQDs (50 mg/kg), C57BL/6 mice were euthanasia, and vital organs were removed. To examine the biocompatibility of PLL-CQDs, the heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney of the mice were collected and stored in 10 % formalin. Then the obtained tissue was decalcified, dehydrated, transparent, embedded, and finally sliced. The tissue sections were stained with Hematoxylin and Eosin staining (H&E Solarbio, China). The histological images were obtained on an optical microscope (Nikon, Japan).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of PLL-CQDs on mediating periodontal bone in periodontitis
ce:section.ce:para: To establish the periodontitis model in animals, twenty-four SD rats (two-month-old, male, Pengyue, China) were anesthetized with sodium pentobarbital (30 mg/kg). Then, the maxilla and mandible of rats were opened and fixed using an elastic band, with the maxilla kept in a more horizontal orientation. Subsequently, the cervical region of the maxillary second molar was ligated with a 4–0 sterile silk thread, tied palatally, and secured to prevent dislodgement for 14 d. Concurrently, the rats were provided with high-sugar drinking water (100 g/L glucose) to induce periodontitis during the feeding period, with daily monitoring of the ligature thread throughout the modeling process. After 14 d, the fixed ligature was removed to accomplish the establishment of the rat periodontitis model. Then, the rats were randomly divided into four groups (n = 6): (1) Sham-operated group (Health), (2) Periodontitis group (Periodontitis), (3) Minocycline (Mino) group, and (4) PLL-CQDs group (PLL-CQDs). Except for the healthy group, the drug was injected every other day in the proximal-middle, middle, and distal second molars of rats, 50 μL (200 μg/mL) PL-CQDs in the PLL-CQDs group, an equal amount of saline in the periodontitis group, and 50 μL of Mino ((Perioclin, Sunstar) paste in the MINO group for 4 weeks. Before execution, the gingival bleeding index (GBI) was measured. All rats were euthanized at a specific time point after the accomplishment of the corresponding experiments.

After 7 d of treatment to analyze inflammation, collecting periodontal tissues and fixing, decalcification, dehydration, transparentizing, embedding and finally slicing. Then the tissue was stained with H&E staining. After 28 days of treatment, tissue sections were prepared as described above and stained using Masson stain and osteoclast stain (TRAP) to assess osteoclast activity and collagen deposition. Periodontal tissue TNF-α and IL-1β levels were also measured by immunohistochemical staining, followed by quantitative analysis expression using Image Pro Plus 6.0 software. To detect the regeneration of alveolar bone, the hard tissues containing maxillary and teeth were scanned by micro-CT (Quantum GX2, Japan) after 4 weeks. Next, the distance between the cemento–enamel junction (CEJ) and the apex of the alveolar bone crest (ABC) was measured at 6 anatomical sites of the maxillary second molars, including the buccal and lingual surfaces (3 points at each side, mesial, mid, and distal area).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Statistical analysis
ce:section.ce:para: All numerical data are expressed as means ± standard deviation. Origin 9.0 software and GraphPad Prism 8 software were used for statistical analysis of the data. Statistical significance was assessed using single-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) with p < 0.05 being statistically significant, * indicates p < 0.05, ** indicates p < 0.01, and *** indicates p < 0.001.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Results and discussion
Successful preparation of PLL-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The preparation of PLL-CQDs was conducted through a convenient one-pot synthesis method utilizing PL hydrochloride as the precursor. HRTEM analysis in Fig. 2
A exhibited the well-dispersed PLL-CQDs with spherical morphology, revealing a lattice stripe with a 0.21 nm spacing corresponding to the (100) plane of graphitic carbon, indicative of a graphite structure within the synthesized PLL-CQDs. This suggests that the (bio)physicochemical characteristics of the PLL-CQDs produced were stable, making them susceptible to rapid degradation [34,35]. The average nanoparticle diameter was determined to be 2.31 ± 0.70 nm (Fig. 2 B), a size comparable to that of previously reported CQDs, confirming the successful synthesis of PLL-CQDs [36–38]. To assess the physicochemical attributes of the CQDs, UV absorption spectra of the PLL-CQDs were recorded, as depicted in Fig. 2 C. The results indicated two distinct absorption peaks at 216 nm (π − π* transition) and 300 nm (n − π* transition) in the PLL-CQDs [39]. Furthermore, the PLL-CQD solution exhibited a light yellow color under visible light and turned gray under UV radiation (wavelength: 365 nm). Additionally, the photoluminescence of PLL-CQDs exhibited dependence on the excitation wavelength, with the highest intensity observed at 480 nm under 380 nm excitation (Fig. 2 D). This is primarily attributed to the quantum confinement effect and the abundance of surface functional groups. For instance, the synthesis of CQDs results in the formation of conjugated π-domains, leading to radiative emission from bandgap transitions within the internal π-region [40]. In addition, the carbon core's surface retains an abundance of polymer chains and functional groups, creating surface defect states that act as trapping centers for emitted excitons [41]. Else, a noticeable redshift was observed with an increase in excitation wavelength.

To confirm the retention of the chemical characteristics of the precursor PLL in the CQDs, the zeta potentials of PLL and PLL-CQDs were measured, revealing that PLL-CQDs effectively inherited the charge properties of PLL (Fig. 2 E), thus demonstrating a positive charge associated with potential antibacterial properties [42–44]. This is mainly attributed to the ability of PLL-CQDs to preserve the abundant amino functional groups of polylysine. The presence of peaks at approximately 3000 cm−1, 1640 cm−1, and 1500 cm−1, corresponding to the stretching vibrations of amino groups, amide I (1647 cm−1), and amide II (1455 cm−1) of PLL [45,46] in the FTIR spectrum, indicated a similarity between PLL-CQDs and PLL, suggesting the effective preservation of PLL's characteristic peaks following the pyrolytic process (Fig. 2 F).

ce:section.ce:section-title: PLL-CQDs induce the differentiation of T cells toward the CD4+ phenotype
ce:section.ce:para:
The nanoparticles, such as PLL-CQDs with an average size of approximately 2.31 ± 0.70 nm, exert their regulatory effect on cells primarily through the endocytosis pathway [47]. Upon internalization by T cells, these small nanoparticles interact with the extracellular structural domains of the T cell receptor complex, notably CD3, leading to altered CD69 expression. This process thereby promotes T cell activation and impacts cell proliferation [48]. To assess the impact of PLL-CQDs on T cell growth, T cells were co-cultured with PLL-CQDs at different concentrations (0, 50, 200, and 800 μg/mL) for 5 d. As illustrated in Fig. 3
A, no significant difference in cell viability was observed between the 0 and 50 μg/mL groups. However, in the 200 and 800 μg/mL PLL-CQDs groups, notably higher cell viability was observed compared to the other groups. These findings indicate that the 200 and 800 μg/mL PLL-CQDs groups facilitated cell proliferation, with T cell viability significantly enhanced in a dose-dependent manner. Overall, PLL-CQDs at these concentrations exhibited excellent cytocompatibility and displayed promising potential for biomedical applications.

To evaluate T cell differentiation, differentiation assays were conducted on the day following the tail vein injection of PLL-CQDs (50 mg/kg) in mice, aiming to characterize the T cell phenotype post-differentiation. Subsequently, the expressions of CD4 and CD8 were analyzed via flow cytometry. As depicted in Fig. 3 B, in the presence of PLL-CQDs, the percentage of differentiated T cells was higher compared to the control group, with a reduction in the proportion of CD4+ T cells relative to the control group. These findings indicate that PLL-CQDs exerted a certain influence on T cell differentiation. Moreover, the spleen coefficients of mice on the first-day post-PLL-CQDs injection did not exhibit a significant difference, suggesting the safety of PLL-CQDs with respect to spleen integrity (Fig. 3 C) [49]. Additionally, the proportion of CD4+ and CD8+ T cells in the PLL-CQDs group was lower than that in the PBS injection group (Fig. 3 D). These results imply that PLL-CQDs could modulate the direction of T cell differentiation, potentially contributing to the reduction of inflammation [33]. That is, CQDs were capable of influencing T cell differentiation, thus contributing to the modulation of adaptive immune responses in inflammatory conditions [50].

ce:section.ce:section-title: PLL-CQDs modulate the phenotype of T cells to promote the osteogenic differentiation of MC3T3-E1 cells
ce:section.ce:para:
To investigate the impact of PLL-CQDs on the osteogenic differentiation of MC3T3-E1 cells, 200 μg/mL of PLL-CQDs was selected because of the significant induction of T cells towards the CD4+ phenotype. ALP is recognized as a crucial marker of early osteogenic differentiation [51]. As shown in Fig. 4
A, the ALP activity of different groups at 7 and 14 d varied. MC3T3-E1 cells in the control, s-P + T, PLL-CQDs, and T Cells groups exhibited weak staining, while cells in the P + T group displayed darker and denser staining. Quantitative analysis of ALP activity aligned with this observation (Fig. 4 C). After 7 d of osteogenesis induction, ALP activity was comparable in the Control, s-P + T, PLL-CQDs, and T Cells groups, with higher activity observed in the P + T group. By the 14th day, ALP activity increased across all groups, with the P + T group maintaining the highest activity (Fig. 4 D). These findings indicate that PLL-CQDs or T cells alone do not influence ALP expression in MC3T3-E1 cells. Instead, ALP expression in MC3T3-E1 cells was enhanced only in the presence of PLL-CQDs affecting T cell differentiation.

The mineralization of MC3T3-E1 cells was assessed by calcium salt deposition using ARS. As illustrated in Fig. 4 B, the P + T group exhibited a distinct large area of red staining compared to the other groups, with the area and intensity of red staining significantly increasing at 21 d relative to 14 d. The quantitative analysis of ARS corroborated the imaging findings (Fig. 4 E/F). Notably, no differences were observed among the control, PLL-CQDs, and s-P + T groups at 14 d, while the P + T group significantly differed from the Control group but not from the T Cells group (Fig. 4 E). By the 21st day, only the P + T group showed a significant difference from the control group (Fig. 4 F). Based on the outcomes of the osteogenic experiments, it was determined that PLL-CQDs did not retain the precursor PLL's capability to enhance osteogenic differentiation. However, undifferentiated normal T cells were found to promote the formation of calcium nodules in MC3T3-E1 cells [52]. In this context, the effect of “P + T” on the osteogenic differentiation of MC3T3-E1 cells may be attributed to the PLL-CQDs-mediated inhibition of CD4+ T cell differentiation through blocking the JAK/STAT pathway, thereby reducing pro-inflammatory cytokine secretion [53]. Furthermore, the upregulation of osteogenic signaling for osteogenesis is closely associated with the Wnt signaling pathway and β-cyclin accumulation in the cytoplasm [54]. PLL-CQDs nanoparticles, which can be internalized through the endocytosis pathway, also have the ability to accumulate β-linker proteins in the cytoplasm and activate the Wnt signaling pathway via nuclear factors of activated T cells, thus further promoting osteogenic differentiation [55].

ce:section.ce:section-title: PLL-CQDs exhibit good biocompatibility in vivo
ce:section.ce:para: Mice were administered tail vein injections of PLL-CQDs (50 mg/kg), followed by pathomorphological examinations of organs such as the heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney to assess structural damage and inflammatory responses triggered by PLL-CQDs. As depicted in Fig. 5 , the examination revealed no significant structural damage to the tissues, with the absence of obvious inflammatory cells indicating the satisfactory in vivo biocompatibility of PLL-CQDs [56].

ce:section.ce:section-title: PLL-CQDs treated periodontitis by reducing inflammation and promoting osteogenesis
ce:section.ce:para:
In rat periodontitis models established via ligature, the impact of PLL-CQDs on the recovery from periodontitis-induced loss of periodontal bone was investigated (Fig. 6
A). Periodontitis triggers inflammation of the gum tissue, with the GBI index determined through a probe bleeding test to assess inflammation levels, where lower values indicate reduced inflammation [57]. In comparison to the periodontitis group (negative control), the notably lower GBI scores observed in the MINO group (positive control) and PLL-CQDs group (Fig. 6 B) suggest that PLL-CQDs and MINO could alleviate periodontal tissue inflammation [58]. However, the GBI indices for these two groups were higher than those of the healthy group, indicating that PLL-CQDs and MINO did not fully restore the diseased periodontal tissues. Controlled by various cytokines, the equilibrium between bone resorption and formation maintains the stability of the alveolar bone volume. Nonetheless, persistent periodontitis triggers the infiltration of inflammatory cells such as macrophages and T cells to the inflammatory site, activating osteoclasts and disrupting this equilibrium. Osteoclasts attach to the bone surface, leading to degradation of the bone mineral matrix and subsequent loss of alveolar bone [59]. Micro-CT analysis (Fig. 6 C) revealed severe alveolar bone loss in the negative control group compared to the healthy rat, confirming the successful establishment of the rat periodontitis model. This was further supported by the increased vertical distance between the CEJ and ABC (Fig. 6 D). Similarly, there was no significant difference between the MINO group and PLL-CQDs group, both of which effectively reduced ligature-induced alveolar bone loss around the maxillary second molar (Fig. 6 D-E). These findings indicate that PLL-CQDs demonstrated significant therapeutic efficacy in treating periodontitis, comparable to that of MINO.

To assess the therapeutic potential of PLL-CQDs on periodontal tissue and inflammation, periodontal tissue was stained with H&E after 7 d of treatment (Fig. 7
A). It was observed that only the periodontitis group exhibited severe infiltration of inflammatory cells, indicating that PLL-CQDs partially reduced periodontal tissue inflammation. After 28 d of PLL-CQDs treatment, Masson staining revealed denser and more organized elastic and collagen fibers in the PLL-CQDs group, while the periodontitis group displayed disorganized and sparse fibers due to inflammation. Additionally, TRAP staining indicated a reduction in the number of osteoclasts with PLL-CQDs treatment. Immunohistochemical analysis of IL-1β and TNF-α demonstrated that PLL-CQDs decreased the expression of these pro-inflammatory factors, positively impacting alveolar bone regeneration [60]. The in vivo treatment outcomes suggested that PLL-CQDs impeded fibrous destruction progression in the animal model. Increased osteoclasts were observed in the periodontitis group due to inflammation-induced alveolar bone loss. Both the PLL-CQDs and MINO groups showed significantly lower osteoclast numbers and downregulated expression of pro-inflammatory factors IL-1β (Fig. 7 B) and TNF-α (Fig. 7 C), leading to alleviation of alveolar bone loss in line with micro-CT results. In a rat model of periodontitis, PLL-CQDs demonstrated therapeutic effects comparable to the clinical drug MINO.

In contrast to prior studies focusing on the application of CQDs to enhance alveolar bone regeneration in periodontitis treatment or combat bacterial biofilm formation in oral infectious diseases, this manuscript represents the first exploration of the interaction between CQDs and immune cells, as well as the evaluation of T cell-mediated immune responses in the context of periodontitis treatment [61–63]. While the goal was generally achieved, the study has several limitations. Firstly, due to the potential in vivo side effects of nanoparticles, a more extensive evaluation of the long-term effects and an exploration of the metabolic pathways of PLL-CQDs in the context of periodontitis treatment will be essential. Secondly, despite PLL-CQDs demonstrating the ability to modulate T lymphocyte responses for periodontitis treatment, further investigation into the uptake mechanism, signaling pathways, and molecular mechanisms underlying the modulation of T cell responses and subsequent effects on osteogenesis is warranted. Thirdly, the sole application of PLL-CQDs may be limited in addressing the multifaceted issues associated with periodontitis, such as bacterial infection, chronic inflammation, and bone loss. Therefore, future research could focus on the functionalization of PLL-CQDs and explore novel applications to enhance their efficacy. Despite these limitations, our findings support the notion that PLL-CQDs can serve as a convenient and efficient platform for treating periodontitis or other immune-related bone disorders.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conclusions
ce:section.ce:para: In summary, PLL-CQDs were synthesized via pyrolysis, resulting in particles with uniform size, good dispersion, and a positive charge. These PLL-CQDs exhibited excellent biocompatibility and increased T cell viability in a concentration-dependent manner. Furthermore, PLL-CQDs suppressed the differentiation of T cells into CD4+ T cells in mice, enhanced alkaline phosphatase expression, and facilitated calcium nodule formation in MC3T3-E1 cells in conjunction with T cells, promoting osteogenic differentiation. In an experimental periodontitis model, PLL-CQDs mitigated periodontal inflammation and reduced osteoclast expression levels, thereby stimulating bone regeneration in periodontitis. Consequently, PLL-CQDs present a promising avenue for periodontitis bone regeneration treatment, offering potential practical utility as an ideal material for periodontitis management.
